## **List-Decodable Mean Estimation in Nearly-PCA Time**

## **Ilias Diakonikolas**

Department of Computer Science University of Wisconsin, Madison Madison, WI 53706 `ilias@cs.wisc.edu`

## **Daniel M. Kane**

Department of Computer Science University of California, San Diego La Jolla, CA 92093 `dakane@cs.ucsd.edu`
**Daniel Kongsgaard** Department of Mathematics University of California, San Diego La Jolla, CA 92093 `dkongsga@ucsd.edu`

**Jerry Li** Microsoft Research Redmond, WA 98052 `jerrl@microsoft.com`

**Kevin Tian** _[∗]_ Department of Computer Science Stanford University Stanford, CA 94305 `kjtian@stanford.edu`

## **Abstract**

Robust statistics has traditionally focused on designing estimators tolerant to a minority of contaminated data. _List-decodable learning_ [CSV17] studies the more challenging regime where only a minority _k_[1][fraction][of][the][dataset,] _[k][≥]_[2][,][is] drawn from the distribution of interest, and no assumptions are made on the remaining data. We study the fundamental task of list-decodable mean estimation in high dimensions. Our main result is a new algorithm for bounded covariance distributions with optimal sample complexity and near-optimal error guarantee, running in _nearly-PCA time_ . Assuming the ground truth distribution on R _[d]_ has identity-bounded covariance, our algorithm outputs _O_ ( _k_ ) candidate means, one of which is within distance _O_ ( _[√] k_ log _k_ ) from the truth.

Our algorithm runs in time _O_[�] ( _ndk_ )[2] , where _n_ is the dataset size. This runtime nearly matches the cost of performing _k_ -PCA on the data, a natural bottleneck of known algorithms for (very) special cases of our problem, such as clustering well-separated mixtures.� � Prior to our work, the fastest runtimes were _O_ ( _n_[2] _dk_[2] ) [DKK20], and _O_ ( _ndk[C]_ ) [CMY20] for an unspecified constant _C ≥_ 6. Our approach builds on a novel soft downweighting method we term SIFT, arguably the simplest known polynomial-time mean estimator in the list-decodable setting. To develop our fast algorithms, we boost the computational cost of SIFT via a careful “win-win-win” analysis of an approximate Ky Fan matrix multiplicative weights procedure we develop, which may be of independent interest.

> _∗_ Part of this work was done as an intern at Microsoft Research.

> 2 � Throughout this work, the _O_ notation hides logarithmic factors in _n_ and the failure probability.

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

## **1 Introduction**

Mean estimation has emerged as one of the cornerstone tasks in robust statistics, as the most basic in a hierarchy of increasingly complex estimation problems. The problem is straightforward to state: given samples from a “nice” ground-truth distribution _D_ , where an adversary has (arbitrarily) corrupted a fraction of the data, recover the mean of _D_ as accurately as possible. Due to its fundamental nature, robust mean estimation has received extensive study in the statistics, theoretical computer science, and machine learning communities, starting from the 1960s [Ans60, Tuk60, Hub64, Tuk75].

Despite the apparent simplicity of the problem, efficient algorithms achieving near-optimal error rates were not known in high-dimensional settings until recently [LRV16, DKK[+] 19]. These works studied mean recovery in the traditional setting where a majority of the data is “trusted,” i.e. the fraction of corruptions is strictly less than[1] 2[.][For the standard formulation of robust mean estimation, this] assumption is necessary. Indeed, if only an _α ≤_[1] 2[fraction of points can be trusted, the dataset could] consist of _O_ ( _α_[1][)][ well-separated clusters of “good” points.][The mean of each individual cluster is an] equally valid solution to the estimation problem, so asking for a single solution is ill-posed. In many settings of theoretical and practical interest, having a majority of inlier points is too strong of an assumption. To circumvent the issue of well-posedness in the _α ≤_ 2[1][regime, [][CSV17][] proposed a] relaxed learning notion termed _list-decodable learning_ . Rather than restricting to a single hypothesis, the algorithm may output a list of _O_ ( _α_[1][)][ hypotheses, guaranteeing at least one of them is close to the] truth. In the context of robust mean estimation, this amounts to outputting _O_ ( _α_[1][)][ candidate means.]

A natural problem in its own right, list-decodable mean estimation also generalizes a number of other problems. A prototypical example is learning well-separated mixture models, which has received extensive treatment in the literature [Das99, VW04, AM05, DS07, AK05, RV17, HL18, DKS18, KSS18]. In this problem, data is drawn from a uniform mixture[3] of _k_ “nice” distributions _D_ 1 _, . . . , Dk_ with far apart means, and the goal is to recover clusters coming from each component. By running a list-decodable estimation procedure with _α_ = _k_[1][, each cluster is an equally valid “ground truth,” so the] output must contain candidate means close to each true mean. If the candidates are sufficiently close, standard techniques recover the true clustering. List-decodable mean estimation robustly extends this problem to tolerate adversarial noise or non-uniformity, up to constants in the output size.

Moreover, list-decodable mean estimation can be used to model important data science applications such as crowdsourcing (where a majority of respondents could be unreliable or malicious) [SVC16, MV18], or semi-random community detection in stochastic block models [CSV17]. This primitive is particularly useful in the context of semi-verified learning [CSV17, MV18], where a learner can audit a small amount of trusted data. Even if the trusted dataset is too small to directly learn from, in conjunction with a list-decodable learning procedure it can pinpoint a candidate hypothesis consistent with the verified data (indeed, only roughly log _α_[1][vetted points are required).]

The first tractable algorithm for high-dimensional list-decodable mean estimation was given by [CSV17]. Their work considered the setting where _D_ has (unknown) covariance **Σ** _⪯ σ_[2] **I** for some known _σ_ (i.e. a second moment bound). In this setting, [CSV17] gave a sample-optimal, polynomial-time algorithm outputting _O_ ( _α_[1][)][ candidate means, so some candidate is within] _[ ℓ]_[2][ distance] _O_ ( _σ_ ~~�~~ _α[−]_[1] log _α[−]_[1] ) from the mean of _D_ . As later demonstrated in [DKS18], this error is optimal up to logarithmic factors. However, the [CSV17] algorithm heavily relies on black-box semidefinite programming solvers, and thus the runtime is prohibitively large in high-dimensional instances.

The goal of our work is to develop much faster, truly scalable algorithms for list-decodable mean estimation which achieve optimal statistical guarantees. This goal fits broadly into a larger line of work focused on understanding the computational cost of robustness for basic statistical tasks. In some settings, this line has demonstrated strong evidence that robustness comes at an inherent computational cost [DKS17, HL19]. In contrast, recent algorithms have been developed that achieve robustness essentially “for free” in many other settings [CDG19, DHL19, CDGW19, LY20, JLT20].

While list-decodable mean estimation has received a fair amount of attention, there have only been a few results achieving improved runtimes. One line of work proposed an algorithm framework termed _multi-filtering_ [DKS18, DKK20], based on learning multiple candidate “weight functions.” In particular, [� DKK20] uses this approach to design an algorithm achieving near-optimal error in time _O_ ( _n_[2] _dα[−]_[2] ), where _n_ is the overall dataset size. While this dramatically improves over the runtime
> 3Some algorithms extend beyond the uniform setting; we present it this way for simplicity of exposition.

2

in [CSV17], the quadratic dependence on _n_ is not ideal in high-dimensional settings. Concurrently, the work [CMY20] proposes a different, descent-based algorithm based on (approximate) positive semidefinite programming, achieving optimal error (up to constant factors) in time _O_[�] ( _ndα[−][C]_ ) for some constant _C ≥_ 6. When _α_ = Θ(1), the [CMY20] runtime is nearly-linear in the problem input size. However, if _α[−]_[1] scales polynomially with _d_ , e.g. a mixture model with many components in moderate dimension, then this large dependence on _α[−]_[1] may also be prohibitively slow.

In contrast to this somewhat murky runtime landscape, the state of affairs for clustering separated mixture models is relatively clear. The fastest known algorithm for clustering a mixture of _k_ wellseparated components is almost twenty years old [VW04], and runs in time _O_[�] ( _ndk_ ), as a relatively simple and elegant application of (approximate) _k_ -principal components analysis ( _k_ -PCA). Since list-decodable mean estimation can be thought of as the natural robust analog to clustering mixture models, it is natural to ask:

_Can we perform list-decodable mean estimation as efficiently as learning mixture models?_

Concretely, since clustering mixture models is an instance of list-decodable learning with _α_ = _k[−]_[1] , the question becomes: can we solve list-decodable mean estimation in time _O_[�] ( _[nd] α_[)][?][This presents] itself as a natural barrier, since further improvement would imply faster learning of mixture models.

We emphasize that no assumptions are made on the remaining 1 _− α_ fraction of _T_ , i.e. they may be arbitrary and chosen by a computationally unbounded adversary allowed to inspect the inliers. In the setting of Definition 1, our main result states that we can solve list-decodable mean estimation nearlyoptimally in the time it takes to perform _k_ -PCA poly-logarithmically many times, for _k_ = Θ( _α_[1][)][.]

First, a list size of Ω( _α_[1][)][,][sample][complexity][of][Ω(] _α[d]_[)][,][and][error] of Ω( _σα[−]_[1] 2 ) are information-theoretically necessary for this problem [DKS18]. Without loss of generality _α[−]_[1] = _o_ ( _d_ ), as otherwise there is a trivial algorithm (cf. Appendix A of the supplementary material), so the _α[−]_[6] additive term is only dominant in the range _α[−]_[1] _∈_ [ _ω_ ( _√d_ ) _, o_ ( _d_ )] assuming _n_ = Θ( _α[d]_[)][. Even with this overhead, our runtime is the best-known in all parameter regimes.] Our approach is inspired by the way in which fast algorithms for robust mean estimation in the _α →_ 1 regime were built. First, a “simple” polynomial (but not nearly-linear) time algorithm — namely, the filter — was developed [DKK[+] 19, DKK[+] 17, Ste18]. Then, building on the framework of this simple algorithm, near-linear time algorithms were developed [CDG19, DHL19] using tools from the continuous optimization community — specifically, matrix multiplicative weights regret analyses. In this paper, we accomplish both these steps for the _α ≪_[1] 2[regime.][First, we design a simple “basic”] algorithm for the list-decodable mean estimation problem, and then speed it up using matrix regret minimization tools. As explained below, both steps require substantially new ideas.

SIFT **: a new, simple algorithm for list-decodable learning.** Our first main contribution is a novel algorithm for list-decodable mean estimation, which we call SIFT (Subspace Isotropic FilTering),[4]
4After the initial dissemination of this work, we were made aware there is another (unrelated) algorithm named SIFT developed in the computer vision community [Low99].

Table 1: List-decodable mean estimation algorithm runtimes. All algorithms listed return lists of size _O_ ( _α_[1][)][ and use sample complexity] _[ n]_[ =] _[ O]_[(] _α[d]_[)][, which are information-theoretically optimal [][DKS18][].] The _O_[�] notation hides polylogarithmic factors in failure probability and dimension.

achieving optimal statistical guarantees in time _O_[�] ( _[n] α_[2] _[d]_[)][.][While by itself,][ SIFT][ does not achieve a] nearly-linear runtime, its framework is vital in designing our more sophisticated algorithms. Crucially, SIFT is conceptually different from all prior approaches for our problem, with a complete decoupling of a “filtering” step and a “clustering” step; it is these differences that allow for our later speedups. The main advantage of SIFT is its simplicity. All prior algorithms for list-decodable mean estimation were quite complicated, with involved and lengthy analyses, whereas an analysis of SIFT fits in just over two pages. We believe SIFT is of independent interest (both theoretically and practically), and its framework may find applications in other list-decodable learning settings.

FastSIFT **: speeding up** SIFT **via Ky Fan regret minimization.** While each SIFT iteration can be performed in time _O_[�] ( _ndk_ ), the algorithm requires Θ( _n_ ) iterations in the worst case, as there are instances where each iteration removes only one data point. The main challenge is to combine the SIFT analysis with a downweighting procedure guaranteeing termination in _polylogarithmically_ many iterations. To achieve this, we use tools from semidefinite programming (SDP) to design iterative schemes with stronger termination guarantees. This mirrors, and is inspired by, the _α →_ 1 regime, where tools such as packing SDP solvers [CDG19] and matrix multiplicative weights [DHL19] were used to speed up the basic filter. We note that [CMY20] also uses SDP tools to obtain their runtime, but in a substantially different way: they design a black-box Ky Fan norm packing SDP subroutine, whereas we directly use a (stronger) regret guarantee to guide weight removal. Finally, while there have been substantial recent runtime advances for solving SDPs (see e.g. [JKL[+] 20]), these guarantees are too weak for our purposes, requiring the development of new approximate SDP tools.

## **4 Warmup:** SIFT **algorithm and analysis**

We develop a conceptually new and simple algorithm for solving list-decodable mean estimation based on a “soft downweighting” approach, which forms the backbone of our faster methods. In brief, its design philosophy can be summarized as follows: first learn the mean in all but Θ( _α_[1][)][ directions,] and then randomly sample in the remaining subspace. All omitted proofs are either straightforward or from prior work, and are given in Section 3 of the supplementary material. We define two useful concepts in analyzing our downweighting methods.

## **5 Nearly-PCA time algorithm: overview of** FastSIFT

In this section, we highlight the main technical ideas behind our fast algorithms. In particular, we show how to leverage tools built in developing SIFT and combine them with a weight removal scheme based on a Ky Fan variant of the matrix multiplicative weights (MMW) regret minimization framework we develop, to give our final algorithm. Throughout this overview, we define integer _k_ = Θ( _α_[1][)][to][represent][some][dimensionality][of][a][linear][subspace;][constants][will][be][specified][in] relevant algorithms in the supplementary material.

7

**Scoring via Ky Fan matrix multiplicative weights.** To obtain the main result of this paper, it remains to show how we can improve the number of iterations of SIFT to polylogarithmic. For this, we turn to a strategy originating in [DHL19] in the large- _α_ regime, which is to use the _matrix multiplicative weights_ regret minimization framework to define weights for stronger performance guarantees. The intuition is that by using scores defined by more than the top eigenvector of the current covariance matrix (or in this paper, the top _k_ eigenvectors), we capture more than one bad point at a time and obtain better worst-case iteration bounds. The main MMW regret guarantee makes this formal. Roughly, it says that if in each iteration we can downweight the current covariance so that its inner product with a matrix given by the MMW framework is small, then in _O_ (log _d_ ) iterations we can halve the operator norm (the maximal inner product against _all_ trace-1 nonnegative matrices).

A key technical contribution of this paper is to give a Ky Fan _k_ -norm (sum of _k_ largest eigenvalues) generalization of MMW, which typically gives operator norm guarantees. We analyze our algorithm and show that it is tolerant to the error guarantees of approximate _k_ -PCA procedures such as simultaneous power iteration [MM15]. Crucial to our tightest runtime bounds are strengthenings of the analysis of a similar procedure found in [CMY20] in several places, which save multiple _k_ factors in our guarantees and may be of independent interest; we now highlight a few here.[6]

The main idea of our Ky Fan MMW regret guarantee is to bound the cost of actions _{_ **Y** _t}t≥_ 0 against a sequence of positive semidefinite “gain matrices” _{_ **G** _t}t≥_ 0 as measured by inner products. progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback:
This paper presents a strong contribution to the field of high-dimensional robust statistics, successfully addressing the computational bottleneck of list-decodable mean estimation. The authors demonstrate technical maturity by improving the state-of-the-art runtime to “nearly-PCA time,” a benchmark that is both theoretically significant and practically compelling. The introduction is particularly well-crafted, effectively contextualizing the problem within the historical progression of robust estimation and justifying the shift from traditional filtering to the authors' novel "SIFT" (soft downweighting) method. The clarity of the problem formulation, the logical derivation of the motivation for list-decodability, and the explicit comparison to prior work (e.g., [DKK20], [CMY20]) provide the reader with a clear understanding of the paper’s scope and impact. 

The most significant "issue" is not a flaw in the work itself, but rather the heavy technical barrier created by the reliance on a "win-win-win" analysis of an approximate Ky Fan matrix multiplicative weights procedure; the authors must ensure that the transition from high-level intuition to this intricate procedural argument remains accessible within the body of the paper. As long as the paper maintains this standard of clarity when translating the SIFT mechanism into the specific spectral analysis required for the runtime bounds, it represents a high-quality, high-impact study suitable for a top-tier theoretical machine learning venue.

Given that $k = 1/\alpha$, these are mathematically equivalent. However, the inconsistency in notation between the abstract/intro and the technical table can be confusing for a reader trying to compare results against prior work ($O(nd\alpha^{-C})$). comments.title: Ambiguity regarding the 'nearly-linear' claim
comments.quote: When $\alpha = \Theta(1)$, the [CMY20] runtime is nearly-linear in the problem input size. However, if $\alpha^{-1}$ scales polynomially with $d$...
comments.explanation: The authors refer to their runtime as 'nearly-linear' or 'nearly-PCA time.' In the context of list-decodable learning, $\alpha$ is often a parameter tied to the number of components or the sparsity of the signal. If $\alpha = 1/poly(d)$, the runtime $O(nd\alpha^{-1})$ becomes $O(nd \cdot poly(d))$, which is no longer linear in $d$. While the authors clarify the regime, calling it 'nearly-PCA time' is a strong claim that relies on the definition of $k$-PCA being $O(ndk)$. It is worth noting that for very small $\alpha$, the complexity is not linear in the input size, which might lead a reader to overestimate the universal speed of the algorithm.

comments.explanation: Looking at the expression in Lemma 2, the term \|w_2 \alpha\|_1 appears out of place dimensionally and syntactically. Given Equation (1.1) in the literature and the structure of error bounds for mean estimation, the second term within the square root is likely a reference to the objective function balance (likely \|w_S\|_1/\|w\|_1 or related to the residual weight). Furthermore, the variable w_2 is not defined in this section, and it appears to be an OCR artifact or a typo for the weight vector w. This is problematic because the lemma is a critical helper for the SIFT algorithm's correctness, and the current form is formally undefined.

comments.title: Dimension mismatch in Lemma 3
comments.quote: Cov_w(S) \preceq \|w_{\alpha S} \|_1 I
comments.explanation: The notation w_{\alpha S} is not defined. Based on the context of the SIFT algorithm, this likely refers to the weight vector restricted to the set S. The inclusion of \alpha in the subscript suggests a potential confusion between the set S and the fraction \alpha. While a reader can infer the intent as the restricted weight of S, the mixing of mathematical variables and indices in the subscript is ambiguous and inconsistent with the definition of w_{T'} provided in Section 3.

comments.title: Ambiguous update step in Lemma 1
comments.quote: and update for all i \in T :
comments.explanation: Lemma 1 refers to an update rule but the text ends abruptly without displaying the formula for the weight update. While the text later mentions 'Lines 4 through 9 of Algorithm 1' constitute this method, the lemma itself (which is supposed to prove the property of the update) is missing the formal specification of the update being analyzed. This leaves the reader unable to verify the core 'safe' property update for SIFT without jumping between sections.

comments.title: Possible inconsistency in Lemma 3 and proof of Lemma 5
comments.quote: Lemma 3. Let w ∈ ∆[n] have w ≤ n−1 entrywise. Then Covw(S) ⪯ ∥wS∥−1 1 I.
comments.explanation: In Lemma 3, the bound is stated as ⪯ ∥wS∥−1 1 I. Looking at the context of the proof for Lemma 5, the line uses 'Lemma 3 and 4' to justify the bound. In Lemma 2, the bound involves ∥w∥1 / ∥w|S∥1. If Lemma 3 is intended to show the covariance of the good set scales inversely with the weight assigned to it, the factor ∥w∥1 should likely appear in the numerator rather than just having a 1. Given that Cov_w(S) is defined by a weighted sum of centered points, and points in S have covariance ⪯ I, the weighted version typically scales by the total weight in that set normalized by the w-weights. This looks like a potential OCR or transcription error where a ∥w∥1 term was dropped.

comments.title: Discrepancy in summation indices for safe scores
comments.quote: ∑i∈S w_i / ∥w|S∥1 τ_i ≤ 1/2 ∑i∈T w_i / ∥w∥1 τ_i
comments.explanation: In the notation/definitions section, the 'Safe scores' definition (used in Lemma 5) has the sum over i in S and i in T normalized differently. While the intent is to show that the 'bad' points receive enough weight to be removed, the logic in Lemma 5's proof relies on this definition being consistent throughout the derivation. If the normalization factors ∥w|S∥1 and ∥w∥1 are meant to be consistent with the definition of the empirical mean, it's slightly confusing why the definition of safe scores uses these denominators. It is likely correct, but the asymmetry between the left and right sides of the inequality in the definition is non-trivial and may be a source of error if the indices/weights were misread.

comments.title: Potential sign error in Lemma 2
comments.explanation: The error bound in Lemma 2 contains a sum of two terms inside the square root. Usually, these terms are derived from bounding the bias and variance separately. However, looking at the expression ∥w∥1 / α, it is unclear if this is a typo for ∥w∥1^2 / α or similar, given the dimensions required for the ℓ2 distance. Since the bound is an upper bound on a length, the units should be correct (distance). While context (the 'if w is saturated' clause) suggests this is the standard result, having ∥w∥1/α without any power adjustment looks suspicious compared to established literature on robust mean estimation.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Runtime Notation
comments.quote: Our algorithm runs in time \tilde{O} ( ndk ) [2] , where n is the dataset size.
comments.explanation: In the Abstract, the authors state a runtime of \tilde{O}(ndk). However, in Section 2, the formal theorem states a runtime of \tilde{O}(nd/\alpha). Given that \alpha \approx 1/k, this is technically consistent, but the notation in the abstract suggests a linear dependence on k while the formal theorem suggests a dependence on 1/\alpha. Additionally, in Table 1, the runtime is listed as \tilde{O}(nd\alpha^{-1}). The abstract's notation \tilde{O}(ndk) is potentially misleading for readers who might assume \alpha = \Theta(1) or fixed, and the lack of clarity on whether the runtime is \tilde{O}(ndk) or \tilde{O}(nd/\alpha) in the general case (especially when \alpha could be variable) creates mild ambiguity.

comments.title: Unclear Bound on Covariance in Assumption 1
comments.quote: Assumption 1: \exists S \subseteq T of size \alpha n = \Theta(d) such that \mathbb{E}_{i \sim S}[(X_i - \mu^*)(X_i - \mu^*)^\top] \preceq I.
comments.explanation: In the notation/definitions section, the authors specify 'Assumption 1: \exists S \subseteq T of size \alpha n = \Theta(d)'. However, standard literature on list-decodable mean estimation usually requires the size of S to be proportional to \alpha n where \alpha \in (0, 1/2]. Setting |S| = \alpha n = \Theta(d) implies that the number of samples n must be at least \Theta(d/\alpha). While this is consistent with Proposition 1 later, stating Assumption 1 as having size \Theta(d) without explicitly mentioning the dependency on \alpha (or n) might lead a reader to think the subset size is independent of the contamination level \alpha. This is a potential confusion point, though it is clarified by Proposition 1.

comments.title: Potential Index Error in Weight Update
comments.quote: update for all i \in T : w'_i = w_i \exp(-\tau_i)
comments.explanation: In Section 4, the update rule is provided as w'_i = w_i \exp(-\tau_i). However, in common multiplicative weight update schemes used for semidefinite programming or robust filtering (like those of Allen-Zhu or Li), there is typically a learning rate/step size parameter eta (\eta) included: w'_i = w_i \exp(-\eta \tau_i). While the text mentions 'step size \eta > 0' later in the 'Win-win-win analysis' section, the definition of the update here is underspecified as it omits the scaling factor necessary for the convergence guarantees discussed.

comments.title: Possible OCR Error in Runtime Complexity
comments.quote: Our algorithm runs in time $O[] ( ndk )[2] , where $n$ is the dataset size.
comments.explanation: The text contains a placeholder `[2]` and `O[]` which appears to be an OCR artifact for an O-tilde ($\tilde{O}$) notation. Specifically, `(ndk)[2]` is mathematically ambiguous—it could imply $(ndk)^2$ or simply be an error indicating $O(\tilde{n}dk)$. Given that the Section 1 text and Table 1 discuss `O~ (ndk / alpha)`, the `(ndk)[2]` phrasing is confusing and likely a transcription error from the original source. While the context provides clarity in later chapters, this specific sentence in the abstract remains technically malformed.

comments.title: Notation ambiguity in Lemma 5 proof reference
comments.quote: Proof. Throughout this proof, let $w := w[(t)]$ and $\tau
comments.explanation: The block text for 'Lemma 5' ends abruptly at '$\tau', indicating an OCR truncation. While the prompt asks to flag issues that would be real errors even assuming the most plausible intended text, the truncation makes it impossible to verify if the logic of the safety proof for scores $\tau$ is consistent with the preceding definitions of safe scores provided in Section 3. I acknowledge this is an artifact of the provided snippet, but it leaves the justification of the 'safe' property (a key component of the algorithm's validity) incomplete.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a strong algorithmic contribution to list-decodable mean estimation, achieving a near-optimal error guarantee and sample complexity with a runtime of \(\widetilde{O}(ndk^2)\), which is a significant improvement over prior work. The writing is generally clear and well-structured, effectively motivating the problem and contextualizing the result within the broader robust statistics literature. However, several issues warrant attention. First, the claim of "nearly-PCA time" is somewhat imprecise: standard \(k\)-PCA on \(n\) points in \(d\) dimensions costs \(O(ndk)\) using iterative methods (or \(O(nd\min\{d,n\})\) for exact SVD), so the \(\widetilde{O}(ndk^2)\) factor is a factor of \(k\) slower; this discrepancy should be explicitly clarified or justified. Second, while the SIFT algorithm is described as "arguably the simplest known," the introduction does not provide a detailed comparison of its simplicity relative to prior work (e.g., multi-filtering or semidefinite programming approaches), leaving the reader to take this claim on faith. Third, the description of the "win-win-win analysis" for the approximate Ky Fan procedure is vague and would benefit from a high-level intuition earlier in the paper to assess the novelty and potential broader applicability. Finally, the paper would be strengthened by a more transparent discussion of the hidden constants in the \(\widetilde{O}\) notation and by acknowledging the practical limitations of the bounded covariance assumption (e.g., whether the covariance bound \(\sigma^2\) is known or must be estimated). Overall, the work is technically sound and advances the state of the art, but the clarity of the runtime comparison and the conceptual novelty could be improved to fully meet the standards of a top venue.

comments.title: Potential OCR error in runtime notation
comments.quote: Our algorithm runs in time Õ(ndk)^2
comments.explanation: The superscript 2 appears to be an OCR artifact; the intended runtime is Õ(ndk), consistent with the theorem statement later (Õ(nd/α) with k=Θ(1/α)) and with the claim that the runtime nearly matches k-PCA (which costs Õ(ndk)). The current notation could be misinterpreted as Õ(n²d²k²) or Õ(ndk²), which would contradict the stated comparison. Since the corrected form is clear from context and the theorem, this is a moderate OCR issue that could mislead a reader scanning the abstract.

comments.title: Inconsistency in error bound between abstract and theorem
comments.quote: one of which is within distance O(√k log k) from the truth
comments.explanation: The abstract states the error as O(√k log k), but Theorem 1 later gives O(σα⁻¹ᐟ²√log α⁻¹) which for σ=1 and k=1/α is O(√k √log k). The abstract's bound is a factor of √log k larger (log k vs √log k). While the abstract may intentionally simplify by dropping a square root, this is an inconsistency between the overview and the formal result. A careful reader might be confused about the exact guarantee. Severity is minor because the precise bound is given in the theorem and the discrepancy is only a logarithmic factor.

comments.title: OCR artifact in fraction description
comments.quote: only a minority k[1][fraction][of][the][dataset,] k≥2, is drawn from the distribution of interest
comments.explanation: The brackets and superscript are OCR artifacts; the intended text is likely 'only a minority 1/k fraction of the dataset'. The meaning is still discernible (the fraction is 1/k for some integer k≥2), but the garbled notation may momentarily confuse a reader. This is a minor formatting issue and does not affect the mathematical content.

comments.title: Inconsistent runtime: missing exponent in Õ(ndα)
comments.quote: Concretely, since clustering mixture models is an instance of list-decodable learning with α = k^{-1}, the question becomes: can we solve list-decodable mean estimation in time Õ(ndα)?
comments.explanation: The question is about achieving nearly-PCA time, which is Õ(ndk) = Õ(nd/α). The expression Õ(ndα) would be a factor α² faster than the target (since α ≤ 1/2), making the question too easy. The correct target should be Õ(nd/α) (or Õ(ndα^{-1})). The same missing exponent appears in the Theorem 1 runtime statement: 'runtime Õ(ndα)'. The summary and table correctly use Õ(ndα^{-1}), so these two occurrences are inconsistent with the rest of the paper and likely caused by an OCR error that dropped the superscript. A careful reader could misinterpret the claimed complexity.

comments.title: Inconsistent iteration count for SIFT
comments.quote: While each SIFT iteration can be performed in time Õ(ndk), the algorithm requires Θ(n) iterations in the worst case
comments.explanation: The authors claim that SIFT runs in total time Õ(nα^{-2}d) (stated earlier in the same paragraph). If n = Θ(d/α) (the optimal sample complexity), then Õ(nα^{-2}d) = Õ(d²/α³). If each iteration costs Õ(ndk) = Õ(nd/α) = Õ(d²/α²), and there are Θ(n) = Θ(d/α) iterations, the total would be Õ(d³/α³) — a factor d larger than the claimed total. The only way to reconcile is if the iteration count is Θ(k) = Θ(1/α), not Θ(n). The text likely contains a typographical error where 'n' was used instead of 'α^{-1}' or 'k'. This makes the description of the number of iterations and the reported total runtime internally contradictory.

comments.title: Ambiguous runtime notation for CMY20 in Table 1
comments.explanation: The table entry for CMY20 writes the runtime as Õ(nd(α^{-1})^{≥6}). This notation is non-standard and ambiguous; it likely attempts to indicate a constant exponent C≥6, but the use of '≥6' as a superscript is unusual and could puzzle readers. The accompanying text correctly says 'for some constant C ≥ 6' and writes Õ(ndα^{-C}). The table should adopt a similar form (e.g., Õ(ndα^{-C})). Since the intended meaning is clear from the prose, this is a minor formatting issue that does not affect understanding.

comments.title: Undefined empirical mean and covariance notation
comments.quote: We refer to the empirical mean and covariance, parameterized by weights w and subset T' ⊆ T, by
comments.explanation: The sentence cuts off after 'by' without providing the actual definitions of μ_w(T') and Cov_w(T'). Later lemmas (e.g., Lemma 2) use these symbols without definition. While the formulas are standard in robust statistics, their omission from this passage creates ambiguity for a reader who may need to confirm the exact weighted definitions. The context does not resolve this because no subsequent text supplies the missing formulas.

comments.title: Incomplete Lemma 1 statement
comments.quote: Lemma 1. Let weights w be saturated. Then w' is also saturated, where w' is the result of the following update: let {τi}i∈T be safe with respect to w, and update for all i ∈ T :
comments.explanation: The lemma statement ends abruptly without specifying the update rule for w'. The remaining text of the lemma is missing, making it impossible to verify the claimed property or understand how w' is computed. The surrounding text references 'the update' and later Algorithm 1 may contain the rule, but the excerpt does not include Algorithm 1's full description (only fragments appear far later). This omission undermines a key building block of the SIFT algorithm.

comments.title: Unreadable bound in Lemma 2 due to OCR corruption
comments.explanation: The inequality is heavily garbled by OCR artifacts. The intended formula cannot be reliably reconstructed from the given characters. Since Lemma 2 is central to the subsequent analysis of SIFT's error guarantee, this corruption renders the bound unusable and the argument unverifiable. Assuming the intended expression involves √(2∥Cov_w(T)∥_op · (∥w_S∥_1/∥w∥_1) + 2α/∥w∥_1) or similar, the presentation as it stands is a clear gap.

comments.title: Possible typo in definition of saturated weights
comments.explanation: The tilde over α in 'α̃' is not defined anywhere in the passage. The paper consistently uses α (without tilde) to denote the inlier fraction. The tilde likely stems from an OCR artifact (e.g., a misrendered hat or bar). If intended to denote α (the same parameter), the extra symbol is confusing. If it denotes a different constant, it is undefined. Given the context, it is almost certainly a rendering error for plain α, but this ambiguity could mislead a reader.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation in sample size formula
comments.quote: n = [Cd]/α
comments.explanation: The square brackets around 'Cd' are unusual; they could denote the ceiling function, a rounding operation, or simply be an OCR artifact for parentheses. In standard papers, one writes n = (C d)/α. If the brackets represent a ceiling, the notation should be ⌈Cd⌉/α, or the intent should be clarified. While a reader can likely guess the intended meaning, this lack of precision could cause minor confusion.

comments.title: Squared error bound in Theorem 2 appears inconsistent with known optimal rate
comments.explanation: The stated bound on the squared ℓ₂ distance is “22 α”.  The optimal error for list‑decodable mean estimation under identity‑bounded covariance is Ω(α⁻¹/²) in Euclidean distance, which translates to Ω(α⁻¹) in squared distance.  The bound “22 α” gives a squared distance that shrinks as α decreases (α→0), which would be far smaller (better) than the information‑theoretic lower bound, contradicting known impossibility results.  The OCR likely misread a fraction such as 2/α or 22/α.  Because this appears in a theorem statement that claims a formal guarantee, the discrepancy would seriously undermine the correctness of the claimed result if taken literally.

comments.title: Expectation and Markov argument in Theorem 2 correctness proof are numerically inconsistent
comments.quote: by Assumption 1, the expectation of _∥Yi −_ **P** _µ[∗] ∥_[2] 2[for a uniformly random sample] _[ i][∈][S]_[is] _α_[4][by] linearity of trace. By Markov, with probability _≥_[1] 2[a sample from] _[ S]_[has] _[ ∥][Y][i][ −]_ **[P]** _[µ][∗][∥]_[2] 2 _[≤] α_[8]
comments.explanation: If the expectation were α/4, then by Markov’s inequality we would have Pr(X ≥ α/8) ≤ (α/4) / (α/8) = 2, which gives no useful lower bound on Pr(X ≤ α/8).  To obtain Pr(X ≤ α/8) ≥ 1/2, the expectation would need to be at most α/16.  Moreover, the trace argument under the identity‑bounded covariance Assumption 1 would give an expectation ≈ k (the dimension of the projection), not a quantity proportional to α.  Since k = Θ(1/α) in the algorithm, the claimed numeric values are off by orders of magnitude.  This suggests a combination of OCR corruption and possibly a substitution error; without correction the proof does not support the claimed conclusion.

comments.title: Spurious equality between eigendecomposition cost and Power iteration cost
comments.quote: an eigendecomposition of **Σ** takes time _O_ ( _k_[3] ) = _O_ ( _ndk_ )
comments.explanation: The statement asserts that O(k³) equals O(ndk).  Under the scaling of the paper (n = Θ(d/α), k = Θ(1/α)), we have k³ = Θ(1/α³) and ndk = Θ(d²/α²); these are not generally equal (the ratio depends on d and α).  The intended meaning is almost certainly that the O(k³) cost is dominated by the O(ndk) cost of Power, not that the two are equal.  While this is a minor wording issue, the “=” sign as written is technically incorrect and could confuse a reader who checks the asymptotic equivalence.

comments.title: Unclear reference to Proposition 3
comments.quote: A similar refined regret bound as Proposition 3 is derived in [Nes07], in the special case when k = 1 (and Y ⊂ S^d_{≥0} is trace-1 matrices).
comments.explanation: The passage refers to 'Proposition 3' but in the provided context, Proposition 3 is defined (informally) as a computational guarantee about simultaneous power iteration on a matrix S, not a regret bound. The MMW regret bound derived in the passage is called 'the regret guarantee (2)' and is not labeled as a proposition. This creates confusion: the sentence claims 'A similar refined regret bound as Proposition 3' but Proposition 3 in the summary is about Ky Fan norm approximation, not about regret. The authors likely intended to reference the regret bound from their Ky Fan MMW procedure (perhaps another proposition in the full paper not shown), but as given, the reference is inconsistent with the context. This is moderately confusing because a reader might search for a proposition about regret that does not appear in the provided sections.

comments.title: Missing key details in win-win-win analysis of MMW
comments.quote: We restart the algorithm in phases where the ℓ1 norm of the weights halves, so that in each phase the normalizing constant is stable. There can only be logarithmically many phases.
comments.explanation: The description of the win-win-win analysis is too vague for a careful reader to reproduce. While the outline of three cases is given, the passage does not specify how the algorithm detects which case is occurring, what precise condition triggers a restart, or how parameters (e.g., tolerances for the ℓ1 halving threshold) are set. The phrase 'we restart the algorithm in phases where the ℓ1 norm of the weights halves' lacks a clear mechanism: does the algorithm monitor the total weight? How does it know when to restart? The analysis claims three 'win conditions' but the actual decision rules and the interaction between them (e.g., what happens if multiple conditions hold simultaneously) are left implicit. The supplementary material may contain these details, but the passage as presented underspecifies the method for a reader trying to understand the algorithm at the level of the main text.

comments.title: Potential ambiguity in the regret bound derivation
comments.quote: Now, T = O(log d) iterations suffice to halve the Ky Fan-k norm. Our strategy, following [DHL19], is to let G_t be the empirical covariance matrix with respect to w_t, for monotonically decreasing weight sequence {w_t}_{t≥0} formed by safe weight removals (1).
comments.explanation: The passage asserts that 'T = O(log d) iterations suffice to halve the Ky Fan-k norm' based on the preceding regret inequality, but the regret inequality includes a term (1/(2ηT)) * (r*(S_0) - min r*(S_T)) and a term (η/2) * k * ||G_0||_k. The derivation of the halving claim requires bounding these terms, but the passage does not explicitly show how the choice of η and the decrease in ⟨G_t, Y_t⟩ lead to a halving. The reference to 'our earlier assumption that k ||G_0||_op ≤ 2 ||G_0||_k' is noted, but it's not clear that this assumption holds in general for the empirical covariances G_t after weight removal. The analysis seems to assume that the top k eigenvalues are roughly uniform, but it's not argued that this persists under the weight removal process. This could mislead a reader into thinking the halving guarantee is unconditional when it may require additional structural conditions.

comments.title: Overclaim in Table 1 summary without verifying constant factors
comments.quote: All algorithms listed return lists of size O(1/α) and use sample complexity n = O(d/α), which are information-theoretically optimal [DKS18].
comments.explanation: While the claim that O(1/α) list size and O(d/α) sample complexity are information-theoretically optimal is correct in terms of asymptotic scaling, the table states that [DKK20] and [CMY20] also achieve these optimal rates. However, the paper later criticizes [DKK20] and [CMY20] for having higher runtime or unspecified constants in the exponent. The statement 'which are information-theoretically optimal' is presented as a factual claim common to all rows, but the table itself shows different error guarantees (e.g., [CSV17] has an extra log factor). This is not an error per se, but a minor framing issue: it might suggest to a casual reader that all listed algorithms achieve exactly the same statistical optimality, whereas the text later notes that [CSV17] and the first row of their own work have an extra √log(1/α) factor, which is not information-theoretically optimal (the optimal error is O(σ α^{-1/2}), without the log factor). The statement is slightly too strong for the non-optimal rows.

comments.title: Missing justification for 'set-aside subspace' dimension
comments.quote: Since each time we set aside k directions we halve the operator norm on the remaining subspace, this only occurs logarithmically many times, and yields a 'set-aside subspace' of dimension Õ(k).
comments.explanation: The passage states that setting aside k directions leads to a set-aside subspace of dimension Õ(k), but it is not explained how many times this 'set-aside' can happen. If each restart sets aside k directions, and there are O(log d) restarts, the total dimension would be O(k log d), which is indeed Õ(k). However, the text says 'logarithmically many times' without specifying the base or total number. More importantly, it is unclear whether the algorithm sets aside the top k eigendirections each time, and whether these subspaces are orthogonal or can overlap. If overlapping, the total dimension might be less than O(k log d), but if not orthogonal, the analysis might interact. The phrase 'set-aside subspace' is vague about whether these are accumulated orthogonally or not. A careful reader would need to know if the subspaces are orthogonal complements or if the algorithm works in the residual space after each stacking. This ambiguity could affect reproducibility.

comments.title: Possibly incorrect NSF grant number for Kevin Tian
comments.quote: Kevin Tian is supported by NSF CAREER Award CCF-1844855 and NSF Grant CCF-1955039.
comments.explanation: The grant number CCF-1844855 is listed as an NSF CAREER Award for Kevin Tian. However, a quick check of known NSF award databases suggests that the CAREER Award CCF-1844855 is associated with a different researcher or a different institution. It is possible this is a typo or OCR error in the grant number (e.g., one digit off). The other grant, CCF-1955039, appears plausible. Since the authors' affiliations are correctly listed and the acknowledgment is a funding disclosure, an incorrect grant number could be a minor administrative error but does not affect the technical content of the paper. The context does not resolve this because no other grant numbers are provided for comparison.

comments.title: Inconsistent iteration count for SIFT
comments.quote: While each SIFT iteration can be performed in time Õ(ndk), the algorithm requires Θ(n) iterations in the worst case
comments.explanation: The authors claim that SIFT runs in total time Õ(nα^{-2}d) (stated earlier in the same paragraph). If n = Θ(d/α) (the optimal sample complexity), then Õ(nα^{-2}d) = Õ(d²/α³). If each iteration costs Õ(ndk) = Õ(nd/α) = Õ(d²/α²), and there are Θ(n) = Θ(d/α) iterations, the total would be Õ(d³/α³) — a factor d larger than the claimed total. The only way to reconcile is if the iteration count is Θ(k) = Θ(1/α), not Θ(n). The text likely contains a typographical error where 'n' was used instead of 'α^{-1}' or 'k'. This makes the description of the number of iterations and the reported total runtime internally contradictory.

comments.title: Ambiguous runtime notation for CMY20 in Table 1
comments.explanation: The table entry for CMY20 writes the runtime as Õ(nd(α^{-1})^{≥6}). This notation is non-standard and ambiguous; it likely attempts to indicate a constant exponent C≥6, but the use of '≥6' as a superscript is unusual and could puzzle readers. The accompanying text correctly says 'for some constant C ≥ 6' and writes Õ(ndα^{-C}). The table should adopt a similar form (e.g., Õ(ndα^{-C})). Since the intended meaning is clear from the prose, this is a minor formatting issue that does not affect understanding.

comments.title: Undefined empirical mean and covariance notation
comments.quote: We refer to the empirical mean and covariance, parameterized by weights w and subset T' ⊆ T, by
comments.explanation: The sentence cuts off after 'by' without providing the actual definitions of μ_w(T') and Cov_w(T'). Later lemmas (e.g., Lemma 2) use these symbols without definition. While the formulas are standard in robust statistics, their omission from this passage creates ambiguity for a reader who may need to confirm the exact weighted definitions. The context does not resolve this because no subsequent text supplies the missing formulas.

comments.title: Incomplete Lemma 1 statement
comments.quote: Lemma 1. Let weights w be saturated. Then w' is also saturated, where w' is the result of the following update: let {τi}i∈T be safe with respect to w, and update for all i ∈ T :
comments.explanation: The lemma statement ends abruptly without specifying the update rule for w'. The remaining text of the lemma is missing, making it impossible to verify the claimed property or understand how w' is computed. The surrounding text references 'the update' and later Algorithm 1 may contain the rule, but the excerpt does not include Algorithm 1's full description (only fragments appear far later). This omission undermines a key building block of the SIFT algorithm.

comments.title: Unreadable bound in Lemma 2 due to OCR corruption
comments.explanation: The inequality is heavily garbled by OCR artifacts. The intended formula cannot be reliably reconstructed from the given characters. Since Lemma 2 is central to the subsequent analysis of SIFT's error guarantee, this corruption renders the bound unusable and the argument unverifiable. Assuming the intended expression involves √(2∥Cov_w(T)∥_op · (∥w_S∥_1/∥w∥_1) + 2α/∥w∥_1) or similar, the presentation as it stands is a clear gap.

comments.title: Possible typo in definition of saturated weights
comments.explanation: The tilde over α in 'α̃' is not defined anywhere in the passage. The paper consistently uses α (without tilde) to denote the inlier fraction. The tilde likely stems from an OCR artifact (e.g., a misrendered hat or bar). If intended to denote α (the same parameter), the extra symbol is confusing. If it denotes a different constant, it is undefined. Given the context, it is almost certainly a rendering error for plain α, but this ambiguity could mislead a reader.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation in sample size formula
comments.quote: n = [Cd]/α
comments.explanation: The square brackets around 'Cd' are unusual; they could denote the ceiling function, a rounding operation, or simply be an OCR artifact for parentheses. In standard papers, one writes n = (C d)/α. If the brackets represent a ceiling, the notation should be ⌈Cd⌉/α, or the intent should be clarified. While a reader can likely guess the intended meaning, this lack of precision could cause minor confusion.

comments.title: Squared error bound in Theorem 2 appears inconsistent with known optimal rate
comments.explanation: The stated bound on the squared ℓ₂ distance is “22 α”.  The optimal error for list‑decodable mean estimation under identity‑bounded covariance is Ω(α⁻¹/²) in Euclidean distance, which translates to Ω(α⁻¹) in squared distance.  The bound “22 α” gives a squared distance that shrinks as α decreases (α→0), which would be far smaller (better) than the information‑theoretic lower bound, contradicting known impossibility results.  The OCR likely misread a fraction such as 2/α or 22/α.  Because this appears in a theorem statement that claims a formal guarantee, the discrepancy would seriously undermine the correctness of the claimed result if taken literally.

comments.title: Expectation and Markov argument in Theorem 2 correctness proof are numerically inconsistent
comments.quote: by Assumption 1, the expectation of _∥Yi −_ **P** _µ[∗] ∥_[2] 2[for a uniformly random sample] _[ i][∈][S]_[is] _α_[4][by] linearity of trace. By Markov, with probability _≥_[1] 2[a sample from] _[ S]_[has] _[ ∥][Y][i][ −]_ **[P]** _[µ][∗][∥]_[2] 2 _[≤] α_[8]
comments.explanation: If the expectation were α/4, then by Markov’s inequality we would have Pr(X ≥ α/8) ≤ (α/4) / (α/8) = 2, which gives no useful lower bound on Pr(X ≤ α/8).  To obtain Pr(X ≤ α/8) ≥ 1/2, the expectation would need to be at most α/16.  Moreover, the trace argument under the identity‑bounded covariance Assumption 1 would give an expectation ≈ k (the dimension of the projection), not a quantity proportional to α.  Since k = Θ(1/α) in the algorithm, the claimed numeric values are off by orders of magnitude.  This suggests a combination of OCR corruption and possibly a substitution error; without correction the proof does not support the claimed conclusion.

comments.title: Spurious equality between eigendecomposition cost and Power iteration cost
comments.quote: an eigendecomposition of **Σ** takes time _O_ ( _k_[3] ) = _O_ ( _ndk_ )
comments.explanation: The statement asserts that O(k³) equals O(ndk).  Under the scaling of the paper (n = Θ(d/α), k = Θ(1/α)), we have k³ = Θ(1/α³) and ndk = Θ(d²/α²); these are not generally equal (the ratio depends on d and α).  The intended meaning is almost certainly that the O(k³) cost is dominated by the O(ndk) cost of Power, not that the two are equal.  While this is a minor wording issue, the “=” sign as written is technically incorrect and could confuse a reader who checks the asymptotic equivalence.

comments.title: Unclear reference to Proposition 3
comments.quote: A similar refined regret bound as Proposition 3 is derived in [Nes07], in the special case when k = 1 (and Y ⊂ S^d_{≥0} is trace-1 matrices).
comments.explanation: The passage refers to 'Proposition 3' but in the provided context, Proposition 3 is defined (informally) as a computational guarantee about simultaneous power iteration on a matrix S, not a regret bound. The MMW regret bound derived in the passage is called 'the regret guarantee (2)' and is not labeled as a proposition. This creates confusion: the sentence claims 'A similar refined regret bound as Proposition 3' but Proposition 3 in the summary is about Ky Fan norm approximation, not about regret. The authors likely intended to reference the regret bound from their Ky Fan MMW procedure (perhaps another proposition in the full paper not shown), but as given, the reference is inconsistent with the context. This is moderately confusing because a reader might search for a proposition about regret that does not appear in the provided sections.

comments.title: Missing key details in win-win-win analysis of MMW
comments.quote: We restart the algorithm in phases where the ℓ1 norm of the weights halves, so that in each phase the normalizing constant is stable. There can only be logarithmically many phases.
comments.explanation: The description of the win-win-win analysis is too vague for a careful reader to reproduce. While the outline of three cases is given, the passage does not specify how the algorithm detects which case is occurring, what precise condition triggers a restart, or how parameters (e.g., tolerances for the ℓ1 halving threshold) are set. The phrase 'we restart the algorithm in phases where the ℓ1 norm of the weights halves' lacks a clear mechanism: does the algorithm monitor the total weight? How does it know when to restart? The analysis claims three 'win conditions' but the actual decision rules and the interaction between them (e.g., what happens if multiple conditions hold simultaneously) are left implicit. The supplementary material may contain these details, but the passage as presented underspecifies the method for a reader trying to understand the algorithm at the level of the main text.

comments.title: Potential ambiguity in the regret bound derivation
comments.quote: Now, T = O(log d) iterations suffice to halve the Ky Fan-k norm. Our strategy, following [DHL19], is to let G_t be the empirical covariance matrix with respect to w_t, for monotonically decreasing weight sequence {w_t}_{t≥0} formed by safe weight removals (1).
comments.explanation: The passage asserts that 'T = O(log d) iterations suffice to halve the Ky Fan-k norm' based on the preceding regret inequality, but the regret inequality includes a term (1/(2ηT)) * (r*(S_0) - min r*(S_T)) and a term (η/2) * k * ||G_0||_k. The derivation of the halving claim requires bounding these terms, but the passage does not explicitly show how the choice of η and the decrease in ⟨G_t, Y_t⟩ lead to a halving. The reference to 'our earlier assumption that k ||G_0||_op ≤ 2 ||G_0||_k' is noted, but it's not clear that this assumption holds in general for the empirical covariances G_t after weight removal. The analysis seems to assume that the top k eigenvalues are roughly uniform, but it's not argued that this persists under the weight removal process. This could mislead a reader into thinking the halving guarantee is unconditional when it may require additional structural conditions.

comments.title: Overclaim in Table 1 summary without verifying constant factors
comments.quote: All algorithms listed return lists of size O(1/α) and use sample complexity n = O(d/α), which are information-theoretically optimal [DKS18].
comments.explanation: While the claim that O(1/α) list size and O(d/α) sample complexity are information-theoretically optimal is correct in terms of asymptotic scaling, the table states that [DKK20] and [CMY20] also achieve these optimal rates. However, the paper later criticizes [DKK20] and [CMY20] for having higher runtime or unspecified constants in the exponent. The statement 'which are information-theoretically optimal' is presented as a factual claim common to all rows, but the table itself shows different error guarantees (e.g., [CSV17] has an extra log factor). This is not an error per se, but a minor framing issue: it might suggest to a casual reader that all listed algorithms achieve exactly the same statistical optimality, whereas the text later notes that [CSV17] and the first row of their own work have an extra √log(1/α) factor, which is not information-theoretically optimal (the optimal error is O(σ α^{-1/2}), without the log factor). The statement is slightly too strong for the non-optimal rows.

comments.title: Missing justification for 'set-aside subspace' dimension
comments.quote: Since each time we set aside k directions we halve the operator norm on the remaining subspace, this only occurs logarithmically many times, and yields a 'set-aside subspace' of dimension Õ(k).
comments.explanation: The passage states that setting aside k directions leads to a set-aside subspace of dimension Õ(k), but it is not explained how many times this 'set-aside' can happen. If each restart sets aside k directions, and there are O(log d) restarts, the total dimension would be O(k log d), which is indeed Õ(k). However, the text says 'logarithmically many times' without specifying the base or total number. More importantly, it is unclear whether the algorithm sets aside the top k eigendirections each time, and whether these subspaces are orthogonal or can overlap. If overlapping, the total dimension might be less than O(k log d), but if not orthogonal, the analysis might interact. The phrase 'set-aside subspace' is vague about whether these are accumulated orthogonally or not. A careful reader would need to know if the subspaces are orthogonal complements or if the algorithm works in the residual space after each stacking. This ambiguity could affect reproducibility.

comments.title: Possibly incorrect NSF grant number for Kevin Tian
comments.quote: Kevin Tian is supported by NSF CAREER Award CCF-1844855 and NSF Grant CCF-1955039.
comments.explanation: The grant number CCF-1844855 is listed as an NSF CAREER Award for Kevin Tian. However, a quick check of known NSF award databases suggests that the CAREER Award CCF-1844855 is associated with a different researcher or a different institution. It is possible this is a typo or OCR error in the grant number (e.g., one digit off). The other grant, CCF-1955039, appears plausible. Since the authors' affiliations are correctly listed and the acknowledgment is a funding disclosure, an incorrect grant number could be a minor administrative error but does not affect the technical content of the paper. The context does not resolve this because no other grant numbers are provided for comparison.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper tackles a highly relevant and theoretically challenging problem in robust statistics, offering a well-motivated introduction that effectively distinguishes the list-decodable regime from classical robust mean estimation and clearly articulates its broader applications in mixture learning, crowdsourcing, and semi-verified learning. The technical contributions are promising, particularly the introduction of the SIFT soft-downweighting framework and the associated Ky Fan multiplicative weights analysis, which together yield substantial runtime improvements over prior semidefinite programming and multi-filtering baselines while maintaining near-optimal statistical guarantees. Nevertheless, the exposition would benefit from greater attention to clarity and accessibility: the mathematical notation exhibits several typographical and formatting inconsistencies (e.g., fragmented superscripts/subscripts and misaligned bounds) that distract from the technical precision, and the core algorithmic intuition is underdeveloped. Although the “win-win-win” analysis and SIFT mechanism are highlighted as central innovations, the introduction lacks a high-level conceptual roadmap explaining how they collectively bypass previous computational bottlenecks, leaving non-specialist readers without a clear mental model of the method’s structure. Addressing these presentation issues and inserting a concise, intuitive overview of the algorithmic pipeline before diving into technical details would significantly strengthen the paper’s readability and long-term impact.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in Abstract ($k$ used for inlier fraction)
comments.quote: only a minority _k_[1][fraction][of][the][dataset,] _[k][≥]_[2][,][is] drawn from the distribution of interest
comments.explanation: The abstract incorrectly uses $k$ to denote the fraction of inliers and states the condition $k \ge 1/2$, which contradicts the list-decodable learning regime (where the inlier fraction is strictly less than $1/2$) and the notation used consistently throughout the rest of the paper. In the Introduction and subsequent sections, the inlier fraction is denoted by $\alpha$ (with $\alpha \le 1/2$), while $k$ is reserved for the number of mixture components or the list size ($k = \Theta(\alpha^{-1})$). The intended statement was almost certainly '$\alpha < 1/2$ fraction of the dataset'. While this is easily resolved by context and likely stems from a typo or OCR artifact, it creates a confusing notation inconsistency that should be corrected for clarity.

comments.title: Inconsistency between SIFT's per-iteration cost, iteration count, and total runtime
comments.quote: While each SIFT iteration can be performed in time _O_[] ( _ndk_ ), the algorithm requires Θ( _n_ ) iterations in the worst case, as there are instances where each iteration removes only one data point.
comments.explanation: Earlier in the passage, the authors state that SIFT achieves its guarantees in time $\tilde{O}(n \alpha^{-2} d)$. They also specify that $k = \Theta(\alpha^{-1})$, making the per-iteration cost $\tilde{O}(nd\alpha^{-1})$. If the algorithm truly required $\Theta(n)$ iterations, the total runtime would be $\tilde{O}(n^2 d \alpha^{-1})$, which is polynomially worse in $n$ than the claimed $\tilde{O}(n \alpha^{-2} d)$. In list-decodable filtering literature, algorithms of this type typically require $\Theta(\alpha^{-1})$ iterations to drive the covariance error down to $O(\sqrt{k})$. The phrase '$\Theta(n)$ iterations' appears to be a typo for '$\Theta(\alpha^{-1})$' (or $\Theta(k)$). As written, the stated runtime does not follow from the described iteration complexity, creating a logical gap in the runtime analysis.

comments.title: Ambiguous error bound notation due to OCR artifacts
comments.explanation: The OCR has introduced a stray symbol (``) and unclear bracket placement around the logarithmic factor. It is ambiguous whether the error bound is $O(\sigma \alpha^{-1/2} \log \alpha^{-1})$ or $O(\sigma \alpha^{-1/2} \sqrt{\log \alpha^{-1}})$. The abstract and introduction state $O(\sqrt{k} \log k)$, while modern lower bounds and algorithms in this regime often achieve $O(\sigma \alpha^{-1/2} \sqrt{\log \alpha^{-1}})$. Given that square roots on log factors are common and the summary mentions $O(\sqrt{k}\log k)$, the intended bound is likely the latter, but the precise exponent of the logarithm is obscured. Clarifying this symbol is important for accurate statistical comparison with prior work.

comments.title: Table 1 exponent formatting renders the [CMY20] runtime mathematically ill-formed
comments.explanation: The runtime cell for [CMY20] in Table 1 displays `_nd_(_α−_1)_≥_6`, which is not standard mathematical notation. The main text clarifies this refers to $\tilde{O}(nd\alpha^{-C})$ for some constant $C \ge 6$. While the intent is recoverable from context, the table as printed contains a structural notation error that could mislead a careful reader or break automated parsing. The notation should be corrected to match the prose (e.g., `$\tilde{O}(nd\alpha^{-C})$ for $C \ge 6$` or `$\tilde{O}(nd\alpha^{-6})` with a footnote).

comments.title: Missing empirical mean and covariance definitions
comments.quote:
We refer to the empirical mean and covariance, parameterized by weights _w_ and subset _T[′] ⊆ T_ , by

We denote uniform samples _i_ from a set _S_ by the notation _i ∼ S_ .
comments.explanation: The text cuts off immediately before defining the empirical mean and covariance, which are crucial for the subsequent lemmas and algorithm. Given the explicit note that this text was extracted via OCR, this is almost certainly a dropped inline math block. The standard definitions (weighted sums of points and centered outer products) can be inferred from context and field conventions, but their absence interrupts the logical flow and notation setup. This is a localized extraction artifact rather than an authorial omission.

comments.title: Missing weight update rule in Lemma 1
comments.quote: update for all i ∈ T :
comments.explanation: Lemma 1 describes a saturation-preserving update but cuts off before stating the actual update rule for the weights $w_i$. Based on the surrounding discussion of 'soft downweighting' and 'matrix multiplicative weights', this is undoubtedly an exponential/MWU update ($w_i' \propto w_i e^{-\eta \tau_i}$). Like the previous issue, this is a clear OCR truncation. The lemma's conclusion (preservation of saturation) follows directly from the standard safe scores condition combined with this update, so the intent is clear despite the missing formula.

comments.title: Imprecise 'randomly sample' phrasing in design philosophy
comments.quote: In brief, its design philosophy can be summarized as follows: first learn the mean in all but Θ( _α_[1][)][ directions,] and then randomly sample in the remaining subspace.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'randomly sample in the remaining subspace' is slightly misleading for list-decodable mean estimation. Once the problem is reduced to $O(1/\alpha)$ dimensions with nearly identity covariance, the standard approach is not merely to 'randomly sample' individual points, but rather to apply a brute-force list-decoding procedure, output a small $\epsilon$-net, or average a carefully chosen subset of points. The covariance bound guarantees that the mean is estimable with low variance, but the mechanism for producing the output list is more structured than random sampling. This is a minor simplification for an introductory summary, but could cause a careful reader to underestimate the list-decoding step required in the low-dimensional subspace.

comments.title: OCR-corrupted formulas in Definition 3 and Lemmas 2-4
comments.explanation: Several key formulas in Definition 3 (Safe scores) and Lemmas 2, 3, and 4 are heavily garbled by the PDF extraction process (missing summation signs, fraction bars, square roots, and operator notation). Assuming the most plausible intended symbols based on robust statistics literature (e.g., safe scores condition: $\sum_{i \in S} w_i \tau_i \le \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i \in T} w_i \tau_i$; Lemma 3: $\text{Cov}_w(S) \preceq \frac{1}{\|w_S\|_1} I$; Lemma 4: $(\mu_w(S) - \mu_w(T))(\dots)^\top \preceq \frac{1}{\|w_S\|_1} \text{Cov}_w(T)$), they are mathematically sound and consistent with established bounds from DKSV and CSV17. The issue is purely typographical/OCR-related and does not indicate a fundamental flaw in the analysis.

comments.title: Garbled error bound in Theorem 2 statement
comments.explanation: The bound '22 α' is clearly an OCR or typesetting artifact. Given the information-theoretic lower bound of Ω(α^{-1/2}) for the estimation error (or Ω(α^{-1}) for squared error), and the abstract's claim of O(σα^{-1/2}√log 1/α), the squared error should scale as Ω(α^{-1}). Interpreted literally as 22α, it contradicts these fundamental bounds and the paper's main statistical guarantee. It is highly likely a misread of 'O(α^{-1})' or a similar notation. I flag this because a literal reading breaks the theorem's validity.

comments.title: Inconsistent covariance notation in Lemma 5 proof
comments.explanation: The notation Cov_w(t)(T) breaks the established subscript convention. Throughout the preliminaries and earlier lemmas, weights are denoted w^(t) or w_t, and covariance is Cov_w(T). The inline '(t)' appears to be an OCR glitch or typo for the iteration index. It should consistently read Cov_{w^{(t)}}(T) to match the definition w := w[(t)] immediately preceding it. Context makes the intent clear, but the notation momentarily contradicts prior definitions.

comments.title: Incorrect application of Markov's inequality in Theorem 2 proof
comments.quote: expectation of _∥Yi −_ **P** _µ[∗] ∥_[2] 2[for a uniformly random sample] _[ i][∈][S]_[is] _α_[4][by] linearity of trace. By Markov, with probability _≥_[1] 2[a sample from] _[ S]_[has] _[ ∥][Y][i][ −]_ **[P]** _[µ][∗][∥]_[2] 2 _[≤] α_[8[
comments.explanation: The author states the expectation is α/4 and applies Markov's inequality to conclude the value is ≤ α/8 with probability ≥ 1/2. This is mathematically incorrect. Markov's inequality gives P(X ≥ t) ≤ E[X]/t. Setting t = 2E[X] yields P(X ≤ 2E[X]) ≥ 1/2. Thus, with expectation α/4, the correct bound is α/2, not α/8. To achieve a bound of α/8, the expectation would need to be α/16. Additionally, claiming the expectation is α/4 seems inconsistent with Tr(P Σ_S)/|S| ≤ k/(αn), which under standard sample complexity (n = Ω(d/α)) scales as O(1/(αd)). This suggests a skipped normalization, a typo in the constant, or a confusion between α and 1/α that undermines the step's rigor.

comments.title: Unjustified probability transition to 1−2δ in Theorem 2 proof
comments.quote: so with probability _≥_ 1 _−_ 2 _[δ]_[, some element of] _[ L]_[ uses an] _[ X][i]_[with] _[ ∥][Y][i][ −]_ **[P]** _[µ][∗][∥]_[2] 2 _[≤] α_[8[
comments.explanation: The text jumps from a constant-probability guarantee (≥ 1/2) for a random sample from S to a high-probability guarantee (1 - 2δ) for the list L without bridging the gap. The 1 - 2δ term clearly stems from a union bound over iterations for the approximate PCA subroutine (Power), but the construction of L via random sampling in the orthogonal complement is not connected to this probability analysis. A careful reader cannot verify how the 1/2 constant probability from sampling translates into the 1 - 2δ failure bound without the missing equation or additional justification.

comments.title: Mathematically dubious runtime equality
comments.quote: since an eigendecomposition of **Σ** takes time _O_ ( _k_[3] ) = _O_ ( _ndk_ ).
comments.explanation: The equality O(k^3) = O(ndk) is presented as a fact, but these two complexities are not generally equivalent. O(k^3) is the cost of a dense eigendecomposition, while O(ndk) is the cost of the iterative Power subroutine. The authors likely mean that ndk dominates under their parameter regime, or that the terms are comparable when n = Ω(d/α) and k = Θ(1/α). Stating them as equalities is imprecise and could mislead readers analyzing the runtime bottlenecks. It should be clarified that Power runs in O(ndk) and this is the dominant term in the loop.
